hero.title: How to Calculate Net Salary (CLT)
hero.subtitle: The complete guide to understanding every cent that leaves your paycheck, mastering the INSS and IRRF tables, and taking control of your financial life.

related_tools.title: CLT Net Salary Calculator
related_tools.description: Calculate your net salary automatically with the 2026 tables.

content.text: It's not how much you earn that matters, it's how much you take home.
content.author: Robert Kiyosaki

content.text: Did you know the average Brazilian can't explain why the amount deposited in their account is less than what was agreed in the contract? The reality is brutal: between the promised gross salary and the money that actually reaches your account, there are two tax guillotines — the INSS (social security) and the IRRF (income tax). And both work progressively, meaning the more you earn, the sharper the blade.

Understanding how these deductions work is not just a matter of curiosity — it's a salary negotiation tool. When you know exactly how much remains from the gross, you can evaluate job offers with much more precision and plan your personal budget realistically.
The Deduction Map: INSS Table 2026

The INSS works through progressive brackets. You don't pay 14% on everything — each portion of your salary is taxed at a different rate. It's like a staircase: each step charges its own rate. See the official 2026 table:

If you earn R$ 4,000, the actual INSS calculation is: R$ 121.58 (1st bracket) + R$ 115.37 (2nd bracket) + R$ 131.66 (partial 3rd bracket) = R$ 368.61. Many people think they pay 12% on the entire R$ 4,000 (which would be R$ 480), but in reality they pay significantly less thanks to the progressive system.
The Second Guillotine: IRRF Table 2026

After deducting the INSS from your gross, what remains enters the calculation base for Income Tax Withheld at Source. Each registered dependent reduces R$ 189.59 from this base. See the 2026 brackets:
The 2026 Game-Changer: Full Exemption up to R$ 5,000

Law No. 15,270/2025 brought a historic change: anyone earning up to R$ 5,000 gross is fully exempt from IRRF starting January 2026. For income between R$ 5,000.01 and R$ 7,350.00, a descending reduction mechanism applies (the closer to R$ 5,000, the greater the discount). Above R$ 7,350, the traditional progressive table applies without any reduction.

The 'Deduction' column is essential. It corrects the fact that the rate only applies to the portion exceeding the lower limit of the bracket. Without the deduction, the tax would be charged on the entire base — which would roughly double the charge.
The Complete Step-by-Step Formula

content.expression: Net Salary = Gross Salary − INSS (progressive) − IRRF (progressive) − Other Deductions

content.text: The calculation order is critical: first deduct the INSS from the gross, then subtract the dependent deduction, then calculate the IRRF on what remains (calculation base). Getting the order wrong completely changes the result.
Complete Practical Example
Maria earns R$ 5,000 gross and has 1 dependent. Here's the step-by-step:
FGTS: The Invisible Reserve

Beyond the deductions, there is the FGTS (8% of gross), deposited by the employer into a linked account at Caixa Econômica Federal. It is not deducted from your salary — it's an additional cost borne by the company. In Maria's example, the company deposits R$ 400.00 in FGTS per month, which she can only withdraw in specific situations such as dismissal without cause, home purchase, retirement, or the anniversary withdrawal program.
Other Common Deductions

Besides INSS and IRRF, your paycheck may include other legal deductions: transportation voucher (up to 6% of salary), meal voucher (up to 20% of the granted value), health/dental plan (co-participation), court-ordered alimony, union contribution (only if authorized), and payroll loan (limited to 35% of net salary). All these deductions must be itemized on your paycheck.
Tips to Maximize Your Net Pay

There are legal ways to increase your net salary without changing jobs: (1) register all dependents with HR — each one reduces R$ 189.59 from the IRRF base; (2) declare medical and educational expenses in your annual return for a refund; (3) contribute to PGBL private pension — up to 12% of annual gross income is tax-deductible; (4) negotiate non-taxed benefits like meal vouchers, health plans, and home office allowances, which are not subject to INSS/IRRF.
